Q1: I tried to keep a steady routine, usually studying about 16 hours spread across the week. I'm a read-write learner, so I spent a lot of time going through written materials and making detailed notes. Honestly, my attendance wasn't great because I found I could learn better on my own terms, reading and re-reading content at my own pace. I'd block out time in the mornings when my anxiety was lower and I could focus better.
Q2: My pattern varied quite a bit depending on how I was feeling emotionally. I aimed for regularity, but my anxiety sometimes threw things off—some weeks I'd be incredibly productive, other weeks I'd struggle to even start. Deadlines definitely pushed me to be more consistent, but I tried not to rely solely on that pressure because it made my anxiety worse.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I needed to check assignment requirements or access reading materials. The gamified tasks actually made me want to log in more often because there was something almost satisfying about completing them. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if there were new discussion posts or updates, even if I didn't have specific work to do.
Q4: Yes, after a few weeks the gamified elements made logging in feel more routine. I started checking in regularly because earning those small achievements gave me a sense of progress when my motivation was low. It became almost like a habit—morning coffee, check the LMS, see what I could accomplish that day.
Q5: The written course materials and reading lists were most valuable to me since that matches how I learn best. I also found the discussion forums helpful because I could read other people's perspectives and contribute when I felt ready, rather than being put on the spot. The gamified tasks were surprisingly useful too—they broke down bigger assignments into manageable pieces which helped with my anxiety.
Q6: I mostly ignored the video content, to be honest. I watched some of it, but I retained information much better when I could read and take notes at my own pace. Videos felt passive to me, and I'd often zone out or feel anxious about missing something important, so I'd just find the written equivalent instead.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see how it connected to my assignments or exams. The gamified elements helped because they gave immediate feedback and made progress visible. I also valued activities where I could work independently and at my own pace—anything that let me control the situation felt more worthwhile because it kept my anxiety manageable.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a relief, honestly. Required ones came with this weight of obligation that sometimes triggered my anxiety, whereas optional ones I could approach with curiosity. I often did optional activities when I was having a good day mentally, as a way to feel productive without the pressure.
Q9: Absolutely, I did most of my actual studying offline. I'd download PDFs and readings, print them out, and annotate them extensively. I had notebooks full of handwritten summaries and notes that the LMS never saw. I also used textbooks from the library and sometimes external articles I found through Google Scholar.
Q10: There were entire weekends where I'd be deeply engaged with course material but never touch the LMS. I'd be working through my printed notes, creating study guides, or re-reading highlighted passages in my textbooks. My best learning often happened with a physical pen in hand, away from screens, which wouldn't show up anywhere in the system.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my standard approach. I'd access a reading once on the LMS, download it immediately, and then work with the offline version for days or even weeks. I'd annotate it, summarize it in my notebook, and refer back to it multiple times—but the LMS would only show that single initial access.
Q12: There were definitely periods when my anxiety spiked and engagement became really difficult. Sometimes it was triggered by unclear instructions or when multiple deadlines clustered together. My resilience isn't great, so when things got overwhelming, I'd retreat and struggle to get back into the rhythm. Those were the hardest weeks.
Q13: My anxiety was the biggest factor affecting my LMS use. When I felt overwhelmed, I'd avoid logging in because even seeing the list of tasks made me more anxious. Fatigue from managing my mental state also meant some days I just couldn't engage digitally, even when I wanted to. I learned to work around this by doing more offline when I was struggling.
Q14: Yes, sometimes I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. My mind would be elsewhere, worrying about other things or just feeling disconnected. I'd technically be on the LMS for a while, maybe watching minutes tick by on a video, but I wasn't actually learning. Those sessions felt hollow.
Q15: I postponed things when they felt too big or when I couldn't figure out where to start. My anxiety made me avoid tasks that seemed ambiguous or where I wasn't sure I'd succeed. I knew they were important, but the fear of doing them wrong sometimes paralyzed me. The gamified structure actually helped with this because it broke things into smaller steps.
Q16: Deadlines were a double-edged sword for me. They created necessary structure and pushed me to complete work, but they also spiked my anxiety significantly. I'd often start working more intensively about a week before a deadline, trying to give myself buffer time. Last-minute cramming was too stressful for me to handle well.
Q17: Feedback motivated me when it was positive or constructive—it validated that I was on the right track. However, waiting for feedback was anxiety-inducing, and sometimes negative feedback would make me hesitant to engage with similar tasks. The gamified immediate feedback was actually really helpful because I didn't have to wait and wonder.
Q18: The course structure with 19 online components was a lot to manage, but having clear modules helped me organize my approach. I appreciated when the structure was predictable because it reduced my anxiety about what was coming next. The gamified elements added a layer of structure that made the whole thing feel more manageable.
Q19: Clear written instructions and well-organized modules made engagement much easier for me. What made it harder was when expectations were vague or when there were too many video-based components instead of text. The gamification actually made engagement easier because it gave me small, achievable goals that didn't overwhelm me.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying to understand the material but struggling to produce work that meets their own standards. Maybe they're reading and watching everything, genuinely trying to learn, but anxiety or perfectionism stops them from submitting. The gap between understanding and producing can be huge when you're battling with yourself.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience somewhat. My attendance was low and I didn't always show high LMS activity, but I was studying extensively offline. I completed most of my assignments and did reasonably well on exams because the learning was happening—just not in ways the system could track. The LMS only sees part of the picture.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access and completion rates pretty accurately. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, the offline work, and the emotional state of the student. They can't tell if someone was anxiously scrolling versus deeply focused, or if someone spent hours with printed materials learning effectively.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm actively thinking, questioning, and connecting ideas—whether that's online or with my notebook. For me, engagement is also about feeling emotionally present and capable of learning, not just logging hours on a platform.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my extensive offline study habits or how much the gamified tasks actually helped manage my anxiety. It wouldn't capture the emotional journey—the days I pushed through despite feeling overwhelmed, or how I developed strategies to work around my low resilience. My engagement was deeply personal and mostly invisible to the system.
